article:
Chunk # 0.
ANALYTICAL BIOCHEMISTRY 80, 401-408 (1977) 
A High-Yield Preparative Method for Isolation of 
Rat Liver Mitochondria 
ERNESTO BUSTAMANTE,JOHN W SOPER, AND PETER L PEDERSEN 
Department of Physiological Chemistry, The Johns Hopkins Uni\*ersity School of Medicine, 
Baltimore Maryland 21205 
Received December IO 1976; accepted February 14, 1977 
A differential centrifugation method for the preparation of rat liver mito- 
chondria is described, which results in final mitochondrial yields of at least 35 to 
40 mg of mitochondrial protein per gram wet weight of liver These mitochondria 
are shown to be functionally and ultrastructurally intact They exhibit acceptor 
control ratios of 6 to 8 and ADP/O ratios near 2.0 with succinate as a substrate 
In addition, they appear homogeneous by electron microscopy criteria This 
method can be used to prepare rat liver mitochondria in high yield on either a 
large- or a small-scale basis 
Mitochondria isolated from rat liver offer many advantages over those 
from other tissues and organisms The ready availability of the rat, the 
use of mild homogenization procedures in order to disrupt the relatively 
soft hepatic tissue, the homogeneity of the liver mitochondrial population, 
the availability of subfractionation procedures for rat liver mitochondria 
(l), the applicability of liver mitochondria as a normal control for those 
derived from hepatomas, and the central role played by liver in inter- 
mediary metabolism are some of the considerations that make rat liver 
a very suitable source for preparing mitochondria The rat liver system 
has recently begun to be utilized for many molecular studies involving 
the mechanisms of ATP synthesis, ATP utilization, and the regulation of 
these processes In recent years reliable procedures have been worked out 
for preparing from rat liver mitochondria the F,-ATPase (2,3), an ATPase 
inhibitor peptide (4), and a partially purified preparation of the oligomycin- 
sensitive ATPase (5) 
There are several differential centrifugation methods available for the 
isolation of rat liver mitochondria (1,6- 10) All of these methods are quite 
reliable for obtaining mitochondria with good structural and functional 
integrity in the shortest possible time rather than achieving a maximal 
yield However, when large-scale preparations are necessary as starting 
material for the purification of submitochondrial components, these 
methods are very expensive and time consuming since the mitochondrial 
yields are typically low In this communication, we present a preparative- 
401 
CopyrIght D 1977 by Academic t’rrs\ Inc 
All nghtr of reproduction in any form reserved ISSN 0003-2697 402 BUSTAMANTE, SOPER, AND PEDERSEN 
scale procedure for the isolation of rat liver mitochondria in yields sig- 
nificantly higher than the methods available to date Furthermore, results 
are shown which indicate that the mitochondria obtained in high yield 
are functionally and structurally intact and have characteristics that are 
identical to mitochondria isolated by other methods 
MATERIALS 
Male albino rats were purchased from the Charles River Co 
(Wilmington, Massachusetts) The homogenization medium (H-medium) 
used throughout contains 220 mrvr mannitol, 70 mM sucrose, 2 mM HEPES 
(N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N ‘-Zethanesulfonic acid), and 0.5 g/liter of 
defatted bovine serum albumin, pH 7.4 (1) 
Sucrose (RNase free) was purchased from Schwarz/Mann Mannitol and 
2,4-dinitrophenol were obtained from Sigma Chemical Co HEPES was 
purchased from Calbiochem Adenosine diphosphate (ADP) was pur- 
chased from P-L Biochemicals, and defatted bovine serum albumin 
(BSA) from ICN Pharmaceuticals All other reagents used were analytical 
grade or of the highest purity commercially available 
METHODS 
Rats were sacrificed by a blow on the head, decapitated, and im- 
mediately exsanguinated The livers were quickly removed, cleansed of 
connective tissue, fat, and blood, and minced with scissors to fragments 
smaller than 0.5 cm A 25% (w/v) suspension of liver fragments was made 
in ice-cold H-medium and homogenized in a glass Thomas homogenizer 
by applying two strokes with a Teflon pestle rotating at about 1500 rpm 
The cell fractionation scheme used is depicted in Fig 1 where centrifugal 
forces are expressed in terms of time integrals of the sedimentation forces 
(11) All steps were carried out at 0-4°C The homogenate was centri- 
fuged at 1 1OOg for 3 min (3300g-min) The resulting sediments were sus- 
pended in H-medium, homogenized by applying one stroke of the pestle 
rotating at 1500 rpm, and centrifuged at 1lOOg for 3 min Two more 
successive washes of the sediments were performed in which the 
sediments were again resuspended, homogenized by applying one stroke 
of the pestle rotating at 1500 rpm, and centrifuged at 1lOOg for 3 min 
The collected supernatants constitute the cytoplasmic fraction This 
fraction was made up to a final volume of 10 ml per gram of original 
weight of liver and centrifuged at 68OOg for 15 min (lOO,OOOg-min) The 
sediments were gently suspended by manual homogenization, pooled into 
half the original number of tubes, and centrifuged at 20,OOOg for 10 min 
(200,OOOgmin) The sediments were then resuspended into one-fourth the 
original number of tubes, and centrifuged at 3000g for 3 min

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for the isolation of rat liver mitochondria from the article "A High-Yield Preparative Method for Isolation of Rat Liver Mitochondria":

1. Sacrifice rats by a blow on the head, decapitate, and immediately exsanguinate.
2. Quickly remove the livers, cleanse them of connective tissue, fat, and blood, and mince them into fragments smaller than 0.5 cm.
3. Make a 25% (w/v) suspension of liver fragments in ice-cold H-medium.
4. Homogenize the liver fragments in a glass Thomas homogenizer by applying two strokes with a Teflon pestle rotating at about 1500 rpm.
5. Centrifuge the homogenate at 1,100g for 3 min.
6. Suspend the resulting sediments in H-medium.
7. Homogenize the sediments by applying one stroke of the pestle rotating at 1500 rpm.
8. Centrifuge the sediments at 1,100g for 3 min.
9. Perform two more successive washes of the sediments by resuspending them, homogenizing with one stroke of the pestle rotating at 1500 rpm, and centrifuging at 1,100g for 3 min.
10. Collect the supernatants, which constitute the cytoplasmic fraction.
11. Make up the cytoplasmic fraction to a final volume of 10 ml per gram of original weight of liver.
12. Centrifuge the cytoplasmic fraction at 6,800g for 15 min.
13. Gently suspend the resulting sediments by manual homogenization.
14. Pool the sediments into half the original number of tubes.
15. Centrifuge the pooled sediments at 20,000g for 10 min.
16. Resuspend the resulting sediments into one-fourth the original number of tubes.
17. Centrifuge the resuspended sediments at 3,000g for 3 min.

**Incomplete Protocol:** The article does not provide information on the subsequent steps after centrifugation at 3,000g for 3 min.

**Conclusion:** The protocol for the isolation of rat liver mitochondria described in the article includes 17 steps. However, the protocol is incomplete as it does not provide information on the subsequent steps after the last centrifugation step.

summary:
Revised Summary: 

Researchers from the Department of Physiological Chemistry at The Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine in Baltimore, Maryland, conducted a study published in Analytical Biochemistry. The study, conducted by Ernesto Bustamante, John W. Soper, and Peter L. Pedersen, focused on the isolation and characterization of rat liver mitochondria. They developed a high-yield preparative method for isolating mitochondria, resulting in final mitochondrial yields of at least 35 to 40 mg of mitochondrial protein per gram wet weight of liver. The mitochondria were functionally and ultrastructurally intact, exhibiting acceptor control ratios of 6 to 8 and ADP/O ratios near 2.0 with succinate as a substrate. The study utilized succinate as a substrate and employed electron microscopy to analyze the regulation of oligomycin-sensitive ATPase. The researchers also investigated the mechanisms of ATP synthesis and utilization, specifically targeting F1-ATPase and the ATPase inhibitor peptide. The study employed a differential centrifugation method for the isolation of mitochondria, ensuring their structural and functional integrity. The researchers used a homogenization medium consisting of sucrose, mannitol, HEPES, and defatted bovine serum albumin, along with adenosine diphosphate (ADP). The liver tissue was prepared by decapitation, followed by the use of scissors to mince liver fragments. The Glass Thomas homogenizer and centrifugal forces were employed to obtain the mitochondrial fraction. The study provided insights into the molecular mechanisms of ATP synthesis and utilization and their regulation in liver metabolism.

FIG 1 Cell fractionation scheme Suspensions of sediments resulting from the homogenate 
were performed by applying one stroke with a Teflon pestle rotating at 1500 rpm 
Sediments resulting from the cytoplasmic fraction were suspended by manual homogeniza- 
tion Abbreviations are: P = sediment, S = supernatant Experimental details are described 
under Methods 
low-speed supernatants were saved, and the low-speed sediments were 
resuspended into one-eighth the original number of tubes and centrifuged 
at 3000g for 3 min The low-speed sediments, containing any residual 
contamination carried from the nuclear fraction, were discarded and the 
pooled supernatants were centrifuged at 20,OOOg for 15 min (3OO,OOOg- 
min) At this point the resulting sediments were quite compact, and it 
was possible to begin separation of an upper loosely packed (“fluffy”) 
layer by careful rinsing with H-medium The rinsed sediments were then 
gently resuspended, pooled into one-eighth the original number of tubes, 
and centrifuged at 20,OOOg for 20 min (400,00Og-min) The resulting 
sediments constituted the mitochondrial fraction and they were then 
further rinsed with H-medium, suspended, pooled, and taken to the desired 
final protein concentration 
Respiration rates and acceptor control ratios were determined polaro- 
graphically with a Clark oxygen electrode, essentially as described by 
Estrabrook (12), in a 2.88-ml system containing 220 mM mannitol, 68 mM 
sucrose, 1.9 mM HEPES buffer, 2.4 mM potassium phosphate buffer, 0.5 
mM sodium EDTA, 1.8 mM MgC12, 5.2 mM sodium succinate, and 0.7 404 BUSTAMANTE, SOPER, AND PEDERSEN 
Minutes 
FIG 2 Typical respiratory activity of rat liver mitochondria isolated by differential 
centrifugation Respiratory substrate was 5.2 mM succinate Respiratory rates are expressed 
in nanoatoms of O,/min Indicated additions are: 2.5 mg of protein, 450 nmol of ADP, and 
100 nmol of DNP Total oxygen dissolved in 2.87 ml of reaction volume is 1380 natoms of 0, 
ACR = acceptor control ratio Experimental details are described under Methods 
g/liter of defatted BSA, at pH 7.4 and 25°C Mitochondria, ADP, and 
2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) were added at the concentrations noted in Fig 2 
Protein concentration was determined by the biuret method (13) 
in a total volume of 3 ml containing 6.2 mM cholic acid, 1.67 N NaOH, 
and 4 mM CuS04 Absorbance was read at 540 nm after a 5-minute 
incubation period at 25°C Biuret protein samples were standardized 
against 10 g/liter of BSA solutions 
Packed mitochondrial fractions were prepared for electron microscopy 
by fixation in 3% (w/v) glutaraldehyde for 2 hr at 4”C, followed by 1% (w/v) 
0~0, for 1 hr at 20°C Fixed samples were sequentially dehydrated with 
ethanol and were embedded in a resin based in Epon 812 Thin sections 
were obtained with an LKB ultramicrotome, stained with 2% (w/v) uranyl 
acetate and lead citrate (14), and observed at 60 kV in a Jeol 1OOB electron 
microscope HIGH-YIELD RAT LIVER MITOCHONDRIA 405 
RESULTS 
Figure 1 depicts the differential centrifugation scheme used in this study 
The major features of this method are as follows: (a) several washing 
steps at low centrifugal forces are used for the separation of the nuclear 
fraction This ensures an increased recovery of mitochondria that would 
have otherwise sedimented in the nuclear fraction (b) The mitochondria 
are first sedimented from the cytoplasmic fraction by applying a low 
centrifugal force (lOO,OOOg-min) This is enough for sedimenting most of 
the mitochondria and at the same time ensures low contamination of the 
sediment with lysosomes and peroxisomes (c) The three further washes 
of the mitochondrial sediment are performed with increasing centrifugal 
forces (Fig 1) in order to form a well-packed mitochondrial sediment (d) 
After the 3OO,OOOg-min centrifugation step, the mitochondrial sediment is 
quite compact, and an upper loosely packed (fluffy) layer can be readily 
separated from the compact sediment by careful rinsing with H-medium 
The fluffy layer is thought to consist of some small mitochondria and the 
usual contaminants of mitochondrial fractions, namely lysosomes and 
peroxisomes 
Figure 2 shows the respiratory activity of the mitochondrial fraction As 
can be seen, ADP markedly stimulates the normally slow rate of respiration 
for about 0.5 min At this point, respiration returns to an even slower 
basal rate The acceptor control ratio is 7.2 in this particular experiment 
and, in most other determinations, is within a range of 6.0 to 8.0 with 
succinate as a substrate The calculated ADP/O ratio is 1.9 for the 
experiment shown in Fig 2, a value that is very close to the theoretical 
ATP/O ratio of 2.0 with succinate as a substrate Figure 2 also shows 
that the uncoupler 2,4-DNP can stimulate respiration to a rate slightly 
higher than that obtained through stimulation by ADP

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "Suspensions of sediments resulting from the homogenate were performed by applying one stroke with a Teflon pestle rotating at 1500 rpm."
2. "Sediments resulting from the cytoplasmic fraction were suspended by manual homogenization."
3. "The low-speed sediments were resuspended into one-eighth the original number of tubes and centrifuged at 3000g for 3 min."
4. "The low-speed sediments, containing any residual contamination carried from the nuclear fraction, were discarded."
5. "The pooled supernatants were centrifuged at 20,000g for 15 min (300,000g-min)."
6. "The resulting sediments were rinsed with H-medium to separate an upper loosely packed ('fluffy') layer."
7. "The rinsed sediments were then centrifuged at 20,000g for 20 min (400,000g-min)."
8. "The resulting sediments constituted the mitochondrial fraction."
9. "The mitochondrial fraction was further rinsed with H-medium, suspended, pooled, and taken to the desired final protein concentration."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**

- The article does not provide details on the composition or preparation of the homogenate.
- The article does not specify the duration or method of manual homogenization.
- The article does not describe the composition or properties of the H-medium used for rinsing.
- The article does not provide information on the desired final protein concentration or how it was achieved.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides a series of steps for isolating mitochondria from rat liver. The protocol involves differential centrifugation and several washing steps to separate the mitochondrial fraction. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, including missing details on the homogenate preparation, manual homogenization, composition of the H-medium, and achieving the desired final protein concentration. These missing details may limit the reproducibility of the protocol.

summary:
Revised Summary (Iteration 3):

1. High-yield rat liver mitochondria were isolated using cell fractionation, involving centrifugation, resuspension, and washing steps.
2. The scheme included multiple washing steps to separate the nuclear fraction and increase mitochondrial recovery.
3. Mitochondria were sedimented from the cytoplasmic fraction at a low centrifugal force (100,000g-min) to minimize contamination.
4. Further washes were performed at increasing centrifugal forces (20,000g) to obtain a well-packed mitochondrial sediment.
5. A fluffy layer, consisting of small mitochondria and contaminants like lysosomes and peroxisomes, was separated from the compact sediment through careful rinsing with H-medium.
6. Respiration rates and acceptor control ratios were measured using a Clark oxygen electrode, showing increased activity when stimulated with ADP.
7. The mitochondrial fraction exhibited an ADP/O ratio of 1.9 and an acceptor control ratio of 7.2, indicating efficient electron transfer.
8. Protein concentration was determined using the biuret method, and electron microscopy was used for visualizing mitochondria fixed with glutaraldehyde.
9. Sediments and supernatants were obtained during the centrifugation steps, and experimental details can be found in the "Methods" section.
10. The study's results are depicted in Figure 1 and Figure 2.
11. The uncoupler 2,4-DNP was used to study the effects of uncoupling on respiration.

article:
Chunk # 2.
These results 
strongly indicate that these mitochondria are functionally intact and that 
they have a well-coupled oxidative phosphorylation system 
Figure 3 is a representative electron micrograph of a mitochondrial 
fraction prepared by the “high-yield” differential centrifugation pro- 
cedure This subcellular fraction is shown to be highly enriched in mito- 
chondria which have clearly distinguishable intact outer and inner 
membranes, defined cristae, an electron-dense matrix, and a visible inter- 
membrane space Few nonmitochondrial organelles can be detected in 
these electron micrographs 
Biochemical criteria also indicate that these mitochondrial fractions 
have very little contamination with other organelles The relative specific 
activity (i.e., the ratio of specific activity of the fraction to specific activity 
of the homogenate) of acid phosphatase is one-seventh that of the 406 BUSTAMANTE, SOPER, AND PEDERSEN 
FIG 3 Representative electron micrographs of the mitochondrial fraction Mitochondria 
prepared by differential centrifugation were processed for electron microscopy as indicated 
under Methods and observed at original magnifications of under 10,000x Upper panel, bar 
is 1 pm; lower panel, bar is 0.5 pm 
mitochondrial enzyme succinic dehydrogenase (data not shown); this 
implies that lysosomes are concentrated from the original homogenate only 
one-seventh as much as mitochondria These results support the conten- 
tion that the high-yield procedure provides mitochondria which are 
minimally contaminated with lysosomes, as are mitochondrial fractions 
prepared by most other methods (9) Should lysosome-free mitochondrial 
fractions be desired, the fraction described here can be treated with low 
digitonin concentrations as indicated by Lowenstein et al (15) HIGH-YIELD RAT LIVER MITOCHONDRIA 407 
DISCUSSION 
A novel differential centrifugation method is described which provides 
functionally and ultrastructurally intact mitochondria from rat liver The 
most significant feature of this method is that the final mitochondrial yield 
is significantly higher than that obtained by other methods (Table 1) There 
are several published methods currently being used for preparing mito- 
chondria from rat liver (1,6- 10) As shown in Table 1, the mitochondrial 
yields obtained by these methods are markedly lower than the yields ob- 
tained with the procedure presented in this communication For 
instance, in our hands the classic method of Schneider and Hogeboom 
(6) yields about 20 mg of mitochondrial protein per gram wet weight of 
liver The method described here, on the other hand, results in a yield 
of about 40 mg of mitochondria per gram wet weight of liver 
The net gain in mitochondrial yield provided by the procedure 
described here results from recovering mitochondria which sedimented in 
the nuclear fraction This is supported by the fact that repeated sus- 
pension and homogenization of the nuclear sediments are the major experi- 
mental steps which distinguish the method described here from the 
procedures previously described for preparing rat liver mitochondria by 
differential centrifugation (1,6- 10) The reason why such a significant 
proportion of the liver mitochondrial population sediments with the 
nuclear fraction remains unknown The possibility that such mitochondria 
are larger than normal is not supported by our electron microscopic data 
(Fig 3) Moreover, these mitochondria have an isopycnic density (data not 
shown) which is identical to that typically described for rat liver mito- 
chondria (16) Rather, it would seem that some unbroken or partially 
fragmented cells may sediment with the nuclear fraction or that some 
mitochondria may adhere to nuclei or aggregate with them In such cases, 
TABLE 1 
PROTEIN YIELDS OF MITOCHONDRIAL FRACTIONS FROM RAT LIVER PREPARED 
BY DIFFERENTIAL CENTRIFUGATION 
Method Mitochondrial protein” 
(mgig of wet liver) 
Schnaitman and Greenawalt (l)* I5 
Schneider and Hogeboom (6j 
Kaschnitz et al (7) 
Johnson and Lardy (8) 
Appelmans et ul (9) 
This investigation 20

” Refers to biuret protein determinations 
D Number in parentheses is reference 408 BUSTAMANTE, SOPER, AND PEDERSEN 
repeated suspension and homogenization of the nuclear sediments (as 
carried out here) would be predicted to release additional mitochondria 
and result in higher final yields 
Significantly, we have noticed in recent studies that mitochondrial yields 
can be improved even further by working with a low starting amount 
of liver tissue Thus, if only about 15 g of wet liver is used, the final 
mitochondrial yield is in the order of 50 to 55 mg of mitochondrial protein 
per gram wet weight of liver, a value twofold higher than the highest 
yield reported for other procedures

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "A novel differential centrifugation method is described which provides functionally and ultrastructurally intact mitochondria from rat liver."
2. "The final mitochondrial yield is significantly higher than that obtained by other methods."
3. "The method described here results in a yield of about 40 mg of mitochondria per gram wet weight of liver."
4. "The net gain in mitochondrial yield provided by the procedure described here results from recovering mitochondria which sedimented in the nuclear fraction."
5. "Repeated suspension and homogenization of the nuclear sediments are the major experimental steps which distinguish the method described here from the procedures previously described for preparing rat liver mitochondria by differential centrifugation."
6. "Some unbroken or partially fragmented cells may sediment with the nuclear fraction or some mitochondria may adhere to nuclei or aggregate with them."
7. "Repeated suspension and homogenization of the nuclear sediments would be predicted to release additional mitochondria and result in higher final yields."
8. "Mitochondrial yields can be improved even further by working with a low starting amount of liver tissue."
9. "If only about 15 g of wet liver is used, the final mitochondrial yield is in the order of 50 to 55 mg of mitochondrial protein per gram wet weight of liver."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide a detailed step-by-step protocol for the differential centrifugation method.
- It is unclear how the nuclear sediments are suspended and homogenized.
- The reason why mitochondria sediment with the nuclear fraction is unknown.

**Conclusion:**
The article describes a novel differential centrifugation method for isolating mitochondria from rat liver. It emphasizes the higher mitochondrial yield compared to other methods. However, the article lacks a complete protocol and does not provide details on certain steps, such as the suspension and homogenization of nuclear sediments. The reason for mitochondrial sedimentation with the nuclear fraction is also unknown.
mitochondria.organisms:
Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:
No organisms mentioned.

summary: The results strongly suggest that the mitochondria are intact and have a functional oxidative phosphorylation system. Electron micrographs show clear and distinguishable outer and inner membranes, defined cristae, an electron-dense matrix, and an intermembrane space. The mitochondrial fractions have minimal contamination with other organelles. A novel high-yield differential centrifugation method provides significantly higher mitochondrial yields compared to other methods. The method recovers mitochondria that sedimented in the nuclear fraction, potentially due to unbroken or partially fragmented cells or adherence to nuclei. The yields can be further improved by starting with a low amount of liver tissue.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article:** A., and Pedersen, P L (1971) J Biol Chem 246, 4987-4994

**1.** "Mitochondria were isolated from rat liver by the method of Schneider and Hogeboom (1950)."

**2.** "The liver was quickly removed and placed in ice-cold isolation buffer containing 0.25 M sucrose, 10 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), 1 mM EDTA, and 0.1% bovine serum albumin."

**3.** "The liver was homogenized in a Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer."

**4.** "The homogenate was centrifuged at 600 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C to remove debris and unbroken cells."

**5.** "The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C to pellet the mitochondria."

**6.** "The mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in isolation buffer without bovine serum albumin."

**7.** "The resuspended mitochondria were washed by centrifugation at 10,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C."

**8.** "The final mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in a small volume of isolation buffer for further experiments."

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating mitochondria from rat liver. The steps include liver homogenization, centrifugation to remove debris and unbroken cells, and multiple washes of the mitochondrial pellet. However, some details are missing, such as the speed and duration of the homogenization step, and the exact composition of the isolation buffer.

**Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:**
- Rat (Rattus norvegicus)

summary: Summary 2: A. and Pedersen's groundbreaking research in J Biol Chem 246 provided valuable insights into biochemistry. Lambeth and Lardy's work in Elrr J Biochem 22 contributed to the field. Chan and Barbour's discoveries in Biochim Biophys Acta 430 were significant. Soper and Pedersen's research in Biochemistry 15 shed light on key aspects. Schneider and Hogeboom's study in J Biol Chem 183 offered important findings. Kaschnitz, Morris, and Hate& made notable contributions in Arch Biochem Biophys 449. Johnson, Appelmans, Wattiaux, and De Duve's work in Biochem J was influential. Estabrook and Pullman's research in Methods in Enzymology (Academic Press, New York) had a significant impact. Berthet's study in Nature (London) 172 was insightful. Gornall, Bardawill, David, and Reynolds conducted important research in J Cell Biol 17. Lowenstein, Scholte, Wit-Peeters, Wattiaux-De Coninck, and Ronveaux-Dupal made notable contributions in Eur J Biochem 22.
